title: The case for and against universal basic income
explanation:
The case for and against universal basic income

Universal basic income (UBI) proposes regular unconditional payments to all citizens. Supporters claim it would simplify welfare systems, reduce poverty and give workers flexibility in a changing labour market. Opponents warn of enormous fiscal cost and reduced incentive to work. Pilot programmes have produced mixed results, partly because short trials cannot capture long-term behavioural effects. The debate ultimately hinges on whether security or conditionality better promotes social trust.

questions.text: UBI means payments that are [*unconditional|secret|temporary only for students].
Supporters say UBI could [*simplify welfare|ban all jobs|end education].
Opponents fear high fiscal cost and less incentive to [*work|travel|sleep].
Pilot programmes have produced [*mixed results|no data|uniform success everywhere].

exercise_sets.title: Exercise 2
exercise_sets.instructions: Choose the correct option for each gap.

questions.text: Short trials cannot capture long-term ____ effects. | *behavioural | musical | weather
Supporters believe UBI could reduce ____.  | *poverty | poetry | parking
The debate concerns security versus ____.  | *conditionality | celebrity | certainty only
UBI could give workers greater ____.  | *flexibility | furniture | fluency only
The article is [*balanced|one-sided propaganda|unrelated to economics].
The central question involves [*social trust|recipe design|film reviews].
Fiscal cost is a concern for [*opponents|supporters only|no one].
